title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Every time you smoke a cigarette, toxic gases pass into your lungs, then into your blood stream, where they spread to every organ in your body.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: A cigarette is made using the tobacco leaf, which contains nicotine and a variety of other compounds. As the tobacco and compounds burn, they release thousands of dangerous chemicals, including over 40 known to cause cancer. Cigarette smoke contains the poisonous gases carbon monoxide and nitrogen oxide, as well as trace amounts of cancer-causing radioactive particles.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: All forms of tobacco are dangerous, including cigars, pipes, and smokeless tobacco, such as chewing tobacco and snuff.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Nicotine is an addictive chemical in tobacco. After you inhale tobacco smoke, nicotine flows through the bloodstream to your brain, where it induces a pleasurable feeling. When you repeatedly expose your brain to nicotine, it becomes desensitized, making you crave more and more nicotine just to feel normal.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking causes death. People who smoke typically die at an earlier age than non-smokers. In fact, one of every five deaths in the United States is linked to cigarette smoking. If you smoke your risk of major health problems increases dramatically, including heart disease, heart attack, stroke, lung cancer, and death from chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking causes cardiovascular disease. When nicotine flows through your adrenal glands, it stimulates the release of epinephrine, a hormone that raises your blood pressure. In addition, nicotine and carbon monoxide can damage the lining of the inner walls in your arteries.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking also raises your risk for blood clots, because it causes platelets in your blood to clump together.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking increases your risk for peripheral vascular disease, in which atherosclerotic plaques block the large arteries in your arms and legs.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking can also cause an abdominal aortic aneurysm, which is a swelling or weakening of your aorta where it runs through your abdomen.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Smoking damages two main parts of your lungs. Your airways also called bronchial tubes, and small air sacs called alveoli.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: With each breath, air travels down your windpipe, called the trachea, and enters your lungs through your bronchial tubes. Air then moves in two thousands of tiny alveoli, where oxygen from the air moves into your blood stream, and the waste product carbon dioxide moves out of your blood stream.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Tiny hair-like projections, called cilia, line your bronchial tubes and sweep harmful substances out of your lungs.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Cigarette smoke irritates the lining of your bronchial tubes, causing them to swell and make Cigarette smoke also slows the movement of your cilia, causing some of the smoke and mucus to stay in your lungs.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: While you're sleeping, some of the cilia recover and start pushing more pollutants and mucus out of your lungs. When you wake up, your body attempts to expel this material by coughing repeatedly, a condition known as smokers cough.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Over time, chronic bronchitis develops as your cilia stop working, your airways become clogged with scars and mucus, and breathing becomes difficult. Your lungs are now more vulnerable to further disease.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Cigarette smoke also damages your LVLI, making it harder for oxygen and carbon dioxide to exchange with your blood. Over time, so little oxygen can reach your blood that you may develop emphysema, a condition in which you must gasp for every breath and wear an oxygen tube under your nose in order to breathe.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Chronic bronchitis and emphysema are collectively called chronic obstructive pulmonary disease COPD is a gradual loss of the ability to breathe for which there is no cure.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Cigarette smoke contains at least 40 cancer-causing substances called carcinogens, including cyanide, formaldehyde, benzene, and ammonia.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: In your body, healthy cells grow, make new cells, then die. Genetic material inside each cell, called DNA, directs this process. If you smoke toxic chemicals, can damage the DNA in your healthy cells. As a result, your damaged cells create new unhealthy cells, which grow out of control and may spread to other parts of your body.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: The most common cancer in the world is lung cancer, with over a million new cases diagnosed every year.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Harmful chemicals in cigarettes can cause cancer in other parts of your body, such as, in the blood and bone marrow, mouth, larynx, throat, esophagus, stomach, pancreas, kidney, bladder, uterus, and cervix.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Cigarette can cause infertility in both men and women. If a woman is pregnant and smokes during pregnancy, she exposes her baby to the cigarette's poisonous chemicals, causing a greater risk of low birth weight, miscarriage, pre-term delivery, still birth, infant death, and sudden infant death syndrome.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Hitting is also dangerous if a mother is breastfeeding. Nicotine passes to the baby through breast milk, and can cause restlessness, rapid heartbeat, vomiting, interrupted sleep, or diarrhea.

title: ANH12071 Health Effects of Smoking
text_en: Other health effects of smoking include low bone density and increased risk for hip fracture among women, gum disease often leading to tooth loss and surgery, immune system dysfunction, delayed wound healing, and sexual impotence in men.
